objective: Read recent Gmail messages, find the ones related to MarianneAI, extract sender intent, and queue valid tasks to None.

message.subject: I want to ask Marriane AI about data about comapny in france

classification.reasoning: Although the email body is empty, the subject line explicitly mentions Marianne AI and expresses a desire to query the platform about French company data. This directly aligns with the project's mission to analyze public data, making it a relevant inquiry or support request.

intent.intent_summary: The sender wants to use Marianne AI to query public data regarding companies in France, but left the email body empty.
intent.requested_outcome: Guidance or capability confirmation on how to ask Marianne AI about French corporate data.
intent.task_description: Follow up with the sender to determine their specific data needs regarding French companies and provide instructions on how to use Marianne AI for this purpose.
missing_information: Exact question or data points needed (e.g., financials, registration status)

assessment.rationale: The email body is empty and the subject line only indicates a general desire to query French company data. Without a specific company name or an actual question, there is no actionable task to queue.
missing_information: Specific company name or identifier (e.g., SIREN)
The exact question or data points needed

message.subject: Oscars 2026 : entre le couac Timothée Chalamet et la remontada «Sinners», une valse des favoris qui donne le tournis
message.snippet: Les articles, alertes et infos Libé à ne pas manquer. Mes newsletters La version en ligne Logo Libération Alerte Libé Le 13 mars 2026 Image Oscars 2026 Entre le couac Timothée Chalamet et la remontada
message.plain_text_body:
Oscars 2026 : entre le couac Timothée Chalamet et la remontada «Sinners», une
valse des favoris qui donne le tournis Les articles, alertes et infos Libé à ne
pas manquer.

Mes newsletters
<https://newsletter.liberation.fr/m/ml/200138/554903/Wcf3vlm3sbUAq2E0BEhEGg1u4knnCbk2a79Jxsgh-Ho=/NSiEzA4rCAMq1S2S6fpkaepiy33N01LOp00M9UbG2OI=/QwZOTF9BbGVydGVfTGliZV8yMDI2LTAzLTE0ACDAA2h0dHBzOi8vbmV3c2xldHRlci5saWJlcmF0aW9uLhrgAXIvd2YvMjAwMTM4LzU3L2ZyL1N0YXJ0LmFjdD9wciLwAGZpbGU9MjIwMjIzNzYmcCggQAZvZmlsZUNvZGU9ZDdkZDE5YTAxNjBmZjkzZWE0NThmZDA1ODYyZTUxNzRhZTMyY2ZlMw==>

La version en ligne
<https://newsletter.liberation.fr/m/ml/200138/554903/Wcf3vlm3sbUAq2E0BEhEGg1u4knnCbk2a79Jxsgh-Ho=/NSiEzA4rCANi2tDfTJkRmZxFzKKndy9RUGYrdr+YE3M=/QwZOTF9BbGVydGVfTGliZV8yMDI2LTAzLTE0ACBAAWh0dHBzOi8vbmUaIABzbCJwAHR0ZXIubGkpEABAEEAAYXRgEKAILmZyL20vdmlldy8yMDAxMzgvNTU0OTAzL1djZjN2bG0zc2JVQXEyRTBCRWhFR2cxdTRrbm5DYmsyYTc5SnhzZ2gtSG89>

<https://newsletter.liberation.fr/m/ml/200138/554903/Wcf3vlm3sbUAq2E0BEhEGg1u4knnCbk2a79Jxsgh-Ho=/NSiEzA4rCAN69PL6kzkuhGb-TFRFQLxZpLKpKhVV3mM=/QwZOTF9BbGVydGVfTGliZV8yMDI2LTAzLTE0>

<https://newsletter.liberation.fr/m/ml/200138/554903/Wcf3vlm3sbUAq2E0BEhEGg1u4knnCbk2a79Jxsgh-Ho=/NSiEzA4rCAN69PL6kzkuhGb-TFRFQLxZn3mKSu7vo6s=/QwZOTF9BbGVydGVfTGliZV8yMDI2LTAzLTE0>

Le 13 mars 2026

<https://newsletter.liberation.fr/m/ml/200138/554903/Wcf3vlm3sbUAq2E0BEhEGg1u4knnCbk2a79Jxsgh-Ho=/NSiEzA4rCAN69PL6kzkuhGb-TFRFQLxZXKDWFCL2ZP8=/QwZOTF9BbGVydGVfTGliZV8yMDI2LTAzLTE0>

Oscars 2026 Entre le couac Timothée Chalamet et la remontada «Sinners», une
valse des favoris qui donne le tournis

Alors que les Etats-Unis sont en guerre, que l’industrie du cinéma est en
plein big bang avec le rachat de Warner, la cérémonie des Oscars aura lieu ce
dimanche. A son approche, les pronostics sont plus embrouillés que jamais.Lire
plus

<https://newsletter.liberation.fr/m/ml/200138/554903/Wcf3vlm3sbUAq2E0BEhEGg1u4knnCbk2a79Jxsgh-Ho=/NSiEzA4rCAN69PL6kzkuhGb-TFRFQLxZcjZIrxw99lk=/QwZOTF9BbGVydGVfTGliZV8yMDI2LTAzLTE0>

Cinéma

<https://newsletter.liberation.fr/m/ml/200138/554903/Wcf3vlm3sbUAq2E0BEhEGg1u4knnCbk2a79Jxsgh-Ho=/NSiEzA4rCAN69PL6kzkuhGb-TFRFQLxZuQgDcKm9fsQ=/QwZOTF9BbGVydGVfTGliZV8yMDI2LTAzLTE0>

«Une bataille après l’autre» Leonardo DiCaprio dans le feu de l’exaction

Dans le premier film d’action de Paul Thomas Anderson, un ex-activiste
artificier est confronté à la disparition de sa fille, tandis que son pire
ennemi, suprémaciste blanc, refait surface. Un blockbuster déjanté et explosif.
Lire plus

<https://newsletter.liberation.fr/m/ml/200138/554903/Wcf3vlm3sbUAq2E0BEhEGg1u4knnCbk2a79Jxsgh-Ho=/NSiEzA4rCAN69PL6kzkuhGb-TFRFQLxZTfJMj9uyGzE=/QwZOTF9BbGVydGVfTGliZV8yMDI2LTAzLTE0>
Cinéma

<https://newsletter.liberation.fr/m/ml/200138/554903/Wcf3vlm3sbUAq2E0BEhEGg1u4knnCbk2a79Jxsgh-Ho=/NSiEzA4rCAN69PL6kzkuhGb-TFRFQLxZm+jG9sQFLfc=/QwZOTF9BbGVydGVfTGliZV8yMDI2LTAzLTE0>

«Sinners» Le film pèche dans le désastre

Mélangeant sans sourciller action et horreur, blues et basses électroniques
dans le Mississippi des années 1920, le film mutant de Ryan Coogler ressemble à
un exercice de style daté.Lire plus

<https://newsletter.liberation.fr/m/ml/200138/554903/Wcf3vlm3sbUAq2E0BEhEGg1u4knnCbk2a79Jxsgh-Ho=/NSiEzA4rCAN69PL6kzkuhGb-TFRFQLxZ-pI99ZDw-NY=/QwZOTF9BbGVydGVfTGliZV8yMDI2LTAzLTE0>
Cinéma

<https://newsletter.liberation.fr/m/ml/200138/554903/Wcf3vlm3sbUAq2E0BEhEGg1u4knnCbk2a79Jxsgh-Ho=/NSiEzA4rCAN69PL6kzkuhGb-TFRFQLxZCDJCCoXAQO8=/QwZOTF9BbGVydGVfTGliZV8yMDI2LTAzLTE0>

«Marty Supreme» Avec Timothée Chalamet, raquette man

Course folle branchée sur 20 000 volts, le film de Josh Safdie est une
évocation pleine de fureur des rêves de grandeur d’un champion de tennis de
table, incarné avec jusqu’au-boutisme par Timothée Chalamet. Grandiose.Lire plus

Les articles, alertes et infos Libé à ne pas manquer.

Mes newsletters

La version en ligne


Le 13 mars 2026

Oscars 2026 Entre le couac Timothée Chalamet et la remontada «Sinners», une valse des favoris qui donne le tournis

Alors que les Etats-Unis sont en guerre, que l’industrie du cinéma est en plein big bang avec le rachat de Warner, la cérémonie des Oscars aura lieu ce dimanche. A son approche, les pronostics sont plus embrouillés que jamais. Lire plus

Cinéma  

«Une bataille après l’autre»  Leonardo DiCaprio dans le feu de l’exaction

Dans le premier film d’action de Paul Thomas Anderson,­ un ex-activiste artificier est confronté à la disparition de sa fille, tandis que son pire ennemi, suprémaciste blanc, refait surface. Un blockbuster déjanté et explosif. Lire plus

Cinéma  

«Sinners»  Le film pèche dans le désastre

Mélangeant sans sourciller action et horreur, blues et basses électroniques dans le Mississippi des années 1920, le film mutant de Ryan Coogler ressemble à un exercice de style daté. Lire plus

Cinéma  

«Marty Supreme»  Avec Timothée Chalamet, raquette man

Course folle branchée sur 20 000 volts, le film de Josh Safdie est une évocation pleine de fureur des rêves de grandeur d’un champion de tennis de table, incarné avec jusqu’au-boutisme par Timothée Chalamet. Grandiose. Lire plus

Cinéma

message.subject: “I just kept thinking, they were almost home”
message.snippet: In this edition of the Weekender: studio lot crickets, the spiritual predecessor to vibe coding, and camel-birds ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏
message.plain_text_body:
View this post on the web at https://post.substack.com/p/i-just-kept-thinking-they-were-almost

This week, we’re wandering empty backlots, remembering a colleague in jewel tones, tracing the maker movement, and admiring ostriches.
THE DISCOURSE
Briefly noted
Oscars night: The Academy Awards are finally here, and everyone seems to agree [ https://substack.com/redirect/ebb78a6a-e6cb-49df-b9d2-bf2653455750?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ] that the night’s biggest awards will be a toss-up between [ https://substack.com/redirect/9cc5e46b-08e0-4263-8447-ebce3a86dc1a?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ] One Battle After Another and Sinners. Elsewhere, Ted Balaker  argues that “the silly ceremony continues to shape culture and discourse in often-overlooked ways [ https://substack.com/redirect/7f02c6f2-67a0-49bf-b8da-e04e204745b7?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ],” whatever its waning viewership might suggest. Speaking of waning viewership, Club Chalamet  defends those Timothée Chalamet comments [ https://substack.com/redirect/1651d087-e755-4a4c-ae20-8d1540e2f586?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ] that some think will cost him Best Actor. And Vince Mancini  has drinking game rules [ https://substack.com/redirect/bd68a153-6180-4529-a521-164a901cc5ea?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ] for anyone looking to spice up what is bound to be a long night.
Cormac vs. Claude: The New York Times shared a quiz asking readers to identify passages from human writers, including Ursula K. Le Guin, Cormac McCarthy, and Hilary Mantel, against AI-written passages on similar themes. Substackers were unimpressed: BDM  writes that given the ongoing piracy issues of LLMs, the quiz is an “exercise in humiliating the writers featured [ https://substack.com/redirect/fd4d83c3-a3f4-4ddb-82c5-4c6ad4b9cd6f?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ],” while Max Read  counters the idea that these quizzes measure what they claim to [ https://substack.com/redirect/4f2ba1a6-6a95-4015-b2a2-f9d99124c6b6?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ]. And M. E. Rothwell  takes apart a McCarthy sentence [ https://substack.com/@merothwell/note/c-225807870?utm_source=notes-share-action&r=48ea6r ] to show that what the quiz describes as ungrammatical or “clunky” writing is actually a thoughtfully stylized representation of how the novel’s characters speak.
HOLLYWOOD
That’s all, folks
As the film industry gussies up for its biggest night of the year, Dara Resnik reflects on the changing industry and what happens when Hollywood, the industry, becomes estranged from Hollywood, the place.
Say Goodbye to Hollywood [ https://substack.com/redirect/e8b03e37-b9dd-491c-ad64-b749f89556cf?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ]
—Dara Resnik  in Dara’s Substack  
After a certain number of cocktails (I’ll let you guess the over/under), several of my friends have recently turned to me at separate dinners, and sighed, exasperated: “It’s over, isn’t it?” They mean Hollywood. And by Hollywood, they mean the glamorous place and the world-famous business that grew in her womb. The bad news is that the answer is yes. The good news is that the answer is also no.
Before the pandemic, I advised film and television writers—students and professionals—from all over the world to move to Los Angeles. That’s where the money was. That’s where the majority of the exciting jobs were. Even growing up in New York City, the Center of Planet Earth (yes, I said it), it was clear to me that if I wanted to write, produce, and/or direct tentpole films or network television, I had to move to LA. Let’s be clear: I really didn’t want to. I had a rent-controlled apartment on 86th and Broadway. I was writing for magazines. I was living a lovely version of an Upper West Side life I coveted as a kid, but I knew if I was gonna Make It, I needed to be in Hollywood.
And I was right. Every wave of young people that moved here in a given year was essentially a college graduating “class.” The people who moved here in ’99, or ’02, or ’06 and on and on, all knew each other in some way. You’d be drinking beer in some stranger’s S’lake backyard one night and realize their roommate was the guy who had the UTA desk next to your best friend, and now he’s an agent, or the young CE you were meeting with was the assistant you used to talk to on the phone all the time when you were a PA. We rose the ranks together, because while jobs out here have always been coveted, there were just enough of them, and we brought each other up alongside us. Vacating a desk in Disney development for a promotion? You knew that girl who’d just left Barry Josephson’s company (uh-huh) would be excited to take it. Need to quit that agency mailroom gig? No problem, the network of “classmates” you arrived with had your back and there was always, within months, a gig around the corner. And while the cost of living was high, it wasn’t nearly as high as it was in the City That Never Sleeps.
On the crew side, if you wanted to learn camera, you could start as a 2nd AC, and climb the ladder the same way. And if you got “stuck” on a rung, either as a mid-level executive, or in a crew position that wasn’t the above-the-line gig you dreamed of, you could still make a living, buy a house, get healthcare for your family, send kids to college on the salary. And the perks were enormous. Sure, maybe you’d be making the same amount of money as a middle school teacher, but you got to be on a studio lot, attend premieres, get paid for being creative in some way. You grew in an ecosystem that was creating one of America’s biggest, most lucrative exports: film and television.
I wish I could explain to my most recent graduate students how thrilling it used to be on a studio lot. How alive it was with movement and sound and creativity. From 2001-2008, I mostly worked at Warner Bros. (first as an intern for Mimi Leder, then for Studio 60 on the Sunset Strip, then Pushing Daisies). Lunch was a trip. At one commissary table, a legendary TV director, at another, a background extra for ER on a break, eating Poquito Mas with a fake nail sticking out of his chest. Around the corner, wardrobe for The West Wing rolling by, around the next, two janitors gossiping in the Stars Hollow gazebo. Everyone was part of something bigger than themselves. While yes, the assholes still thrived because welcome to humanity, the feeling of being part of a creative community was pervasive. You couldn’t help but look wide-eyed at your colleagues sometimes and go, “Guys, we’re really DOING IT!”
Sadly, anyone paying attention is finally admitting that has changed. Production has fled. There are fewer buyers, fewer development jobs. The studio lots are devoid of life. I recently had an in-person meeting at the Universal Studios lot, which is huge, and when I inevitably got lost, as I always have, I looked around to ask someone, ANYONE for directions, and I think I literally heard crickets. I was alone. It was eerie. Of course, there are a few productions here and there. But it’s not like it was. And it never will be again. Things change.
Keep reading [ https://substack.com/redirect/e8b03e37-b9dd-491c-ad64-b749f89556cf?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ]
ANIMATION
IN MEMORIAM
“They were almost home”
Dia Lupo reflects on a glamorous coworker, her figure-skating daughters, and the shocking airplane crash that took them all.
To be beautiful, to be memorable [ https://substack.com/redirect/ead74483-1a7e-4db5-ab9c-b861ff7b73f7?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ]
—Dia Lupo  in Broke But Moisturized [ https://substack.com/redirect/4130e91d-e0e5-415b-be8f-6f3094ee2a79?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ]
“Women want to be loved like roses. They spend hours perfecting their eyebrows and toes and inventing irresistible curls that fall by accident down the back of their necks from otherwise austere hair-dos. They want their lover to remember the way they held a glass. They want to haunt.”
―Eve Babitz, Slow Days, Fast Company
Before they were called “Town Hall” meetings, they were called “All Hands.” At some point HR made us change the name because “not everyone has hands.” Believe all 60 of us had both hands but anyway, the point was to get the whole marketing team in a room once a quarter to exchange personality test results and pretend to know what EBITDA means, pretend we control such profitability metrics through Pinterest ads.
In advance of each Town Hall, leadership would pick a few people for these “get to know the team” panels. The questions were personal, and yet everyone’s answers always circled back to work. The host would ask, “Who’s on your Spotify Wrapped this year?” and some middle manager would gush, “Like alllll classical because it helps me focus!” SHUT THE FUCK UP. JUST SAY TAYLOR SWIFT AND WALK THOSE DUSTY ROTHYS BACK TO YOUR CUBICLE.
I remember the last Town Hall I attended before changing teams. We broke for lunch which was, invariably, a taco bar for gringos on SSRIs. Then it was panel time.
Donna Livingston perched upon a stool in her signature glam. Her long, shiny red hair in loose curls; Disney princess eyes with 100 coats of mascara; berry lip. She embodied the bygone elegance of mid-tier city office life, the kind of woman who called a shirt a “blouse” and pants “slacks.” That day she wore black slacks and a deep-pink-hued blouse. I thought to myself, Donna loves a jewel tone. Gotta respect a woman who knows what looks good on her. I’d only ever seen Donna in sapphire and emerald and this shade of pink; it was the color of love refracting off the edges of a ruby.
Donna worked remotely from DC. We were all used to admiring her through a screen, her dedication to Getting Ready every day to work from home. Always warm, always smiling. To see her up there on the panel reminded me that those intangibles like “grace” and “poise” can’t be fully apprehended via Microsoft Teams. Real-life Donna was just so… lovely. That’s the word. Lovely.
At one point, the panelists were asked about their dream jobs. Everyone got fidgety because what on earth would you do if not write website copy for B2B telecom?
They passed Donna the mic and she turned the color of her shirt.
“I would be a figure skater,“ she confessed, her voice shaky and girlish. “My girls skate and it’s the biggest part of our lives.”
Everybody loved Donna, and that was the first time most of us got a glimpse into her personal life. I must have been in a million meetings with her and she never once mentioned this thing that colored a sterile conference room with charm and wonder and truth. And then it clicked for me: that Donna’s glam might be an extension of her passion for skating. It can be a lovely thing, to look the part; it crystallizes your image in the hearts and minds of everyone who meets you.
On January 29, 2025, American Airlines Flight 5342 collided with a US Army helicopter over the Potomac River in Washington D.C. It was a small flight, 64 people between the crew and passengers, 28 of them returning from the U.S. Figure Skating Championships training camp in Wichita, Kansas. Donna Livingston died alongside her husband Peter and their daughters, Olympic hopefuls Everly, 14, and Alydia, 11, who were known online as The Ice Skating Sisters.
I was working from home when my friend called and broke the news. “The whole office is a mess. Everyone’s crying and leaving for the day,” she said. Meanwhile, our friend Sarah had just taken a job under Donna. Sarah was in California for a conference where Donna was to meet her when she returned from Wichita. She was texting with Donna just before she boarded Flight 5342, wished her safe travels and everything.
What are the odds of knowing someone who dies in a 64-person plane crash? I was too catatonic for statistics but just responsive and incredulous enough to watch the video footage over and over and over. The helicopter looks like it’s being pulled directly into the plane by some invisible force, something bigger and more sinister than human error. The aircrafts collide into a magnificent ball of fire suspended in the night sky. I just kept thinking, they were almost home.
Keep reading [ https://substack.com/redirect/ead74483-1a7e-4db5-ab9c-b861ff7b73f7?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ]
PHOTOGRAPHY
TECHNOLOGY
Of slop and crapjects
Sachin traces the arc from 3D-printed crapjects to vibe-coded slop and proposes a more sustainable way to think about what all this building actually produces.
Will vibe coding end like the maker movement? [ https://substack.com/redirect/ca0d7431-8b53-46ca-ac77-706a0321ba8f?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ]
—Sachin  in Technically  
Whenever a new technology arrives, the impulse is to treat it as something that has never existed before. A clean break from everything that came prior. I catch myself doing this with vibe coding constantly, and I see it everywhere around me. But the most useful lens for understanding a new phenomenon is almost never the phenomenon itself. You want something adjacent, close enough to share structural similarities but removed enough to see clearly. It’s on the lookout for something like this that I started reading more about the Maker Movement of ~2005-2015.
The Maker Movement was the spiritual predecessor to vibe coding. The parallels are hard to miss. Vibe coding has slop. The Maker Movement had crapjects, a term the community coined for 3D-printed objects that served no purpose beyond proving you could extrude plastic into a shape. The Claude Code of that era was a $200 printer from Monoprice and a breadboard.
The scene around making produced what were probably the first internet-native network intellectuals. Chris Anderson (who wrote the widely-read piece about the long tail) left his editor-in-chief role at Wired to start a robotics company called 3D Robotics. Cory Doctorow wrote Makers, a sci-fi novel based around characters who are hacking hardware and business models to survive in a world where everything is falling apart. These were people who gained influence by participating visibly in a making culture and writing about what it meant.
A lot of the intellectual energy of the AI era orbits around AGI: when it arrives, what it’ll do to jobs, whether it will be aligned. The Maker Movement had its own gravitational center, and it was the idea that making physical things with your hands could produce an internal transformation. You would become more creative, more entrepreneurial, more self-reliant. The object you made mattered less than what the act of making did to you.
In 2018, the media scholar Fred Turner published a paper that put this ideology under a microscope. His argument was that the Maker Movement had reinvented the theology of the Western Frontier for the digital age.
The specifics of seventeenth-century Puritanism are obviously gone. Nobody at a Maker Faire was talking about predestination. But Turner traced the literary forms and the millenarian structure—the belief that a great transformation is coming, and that individual discipline will determine who makes it through. In the Maker narrative, the American landscape is economically barren. Jobs have disappeared. Institutions have failed you. And in this wilderness, the lone individual searches inside themselves for signs of the entrepreneurial spirit, the creative spark, evidence that they are among the elect who will build their way to salvation.
Turner’s observation extends well beyond 3D printers. You can trace this same pattern through almost every hobbyist technology scene of the past fifty years. Homebrew computer clubs in the 1970s. Punk zines in the 1980s. The early web in the 1990s. Each one developed a community of practice—what Brian Eno would call a “scenius”—where people played with tools that the mainstream considered toys. Each one generated its own salvation narrative: master this tool, transform yourself, become the kind of person who builds the future.
And each one operated with a useful kind of slack. The tools were unproductive on purpose. Nobody expected your Arduino project to ship to customers. Nobody expected your homebrew computer to compete with IBM. The whole point was that you had permission to fuck around, and the finding-out happened gradually, through play, over years. This is where the old Silicon Valley adage comes from: “What smart people do on the weekends, everyone else will do during the week in ten years.”
Keep reading [ https://substack.com/redirect/ca0d7431-8b53-46ca-ac77-706a0321ba8f?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ]
PAINTING
ANIMALS
Birds of a feather
In which Jess Nash defends flightless birds as evolutionary pioneers, rather than evolutionary mistakes.
She Rose Under My Heart the Ostrich-God [ https://substack.com/redirect/a0416ab5-4a83-4ab9-8c93-896de8a91dd8?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ]
—Jess Nash  in All Moving Parts  
The 19th-century Japanese master artist Kawanabe Kyōsai is well known for imaginative and satirical visions carried out in energetic, free brushwork. Between political caricatures, animals, ghosts, and acrobats, his scenes favour movement and lightness. It’s no surprise that he often painted birds.
What better subjects to express liberation and motion? Kyōsai’s soaring eagles, ravens, and songbirds share their substance with moving, changing water and buoyant leaves. Their bodies are as free and flexible as the wind. Air passes through their feathers.
Furthest from this lightness among birds is the ostrich—three hundred pounds and fully grounded. Though they share a class, an ostrich would never fit into a Kyōsai picture like a raven or an eagle does.
This is a thick and gawky animal, strutting on heavy legs, with feathers like hair draping thickly over the bulky body and spraying out at the tail. Up the curve of the neck, there are long black eyelashes, big black eyes, and big black nostrils set into a squat beak. Besides the beak and feathers, not much about the ostrich really looks birdlike at all. They have wings, of course, but these are instrumental in running, not flying.
“In some points it resembles a bird, in others a quadruped,” Aristotle wrote of the ostrich in the 4th-century B.C.E. biological study Parts of Animals. He could make no clear sense of what he observed: feathers and hair, two bird legs and a quadruped’s size, wings and no flight. In his taxonomy of animals, the ostrich was ultimately neither avian nor beast. It was classifiable only as “intermediate”. Indeed, many languages reflect a hybrid view of the ostrich: the Greek term strouthokámilos, the Persian shotormorq, the Turkish devekuşu, and the Chinese tuóniǎo each describe a “camel-bird.”
Though post-Aristotle Jewish and Islamic scholars viewed the ostrich unambiguously as a bird, it was clearly not like other birds. It ate strange things; it built unusual nests; most pressingly, it didn’t fly. In Job 39:13-17, God speaks: “The wings of the ostrich wave proudly / But are her wings and pinions like the kindly stork’s?” The stork has wings that, as biology professor Joel Duff reads, “are beautiful and enable flight.” Not the ostrich.
We on foot have always coveted the wide space of the sky, and the natural ease with which birds navigate it. A bird seems to be a light and free spirit, unconfined by its nature. The caging of a bird is a serious cruelty, and doves soar at weddings. A winged human is nothing less than an angel.
(Burst the wild storm? above it thou ascended’st, / And rested on the sky, thy slave that cradled thee) / Now a blue point, far, far in heaven floating
—Walt Whitman (To the Man-of-War-Bird)
“The human fascination with flight seems to be associated with our desire for the pure transcendence of art,” writes poet Rosemarie Corlett. Leonardo da Vinci, who worked obsessively at building a flying machine, loved birds; he used to buy them at the market so that he could set them free. Wilbur Wright, of the brothers who finally saw the dream of aviation through, wrote:
The desire to fly is an idea handed down to us by our ancestors who, in their grueling travels across trackless lands in prehistoric times, looked enviously on the birds soaring freely through space, at full speed, above all obstacles, on the infinite highway of the air.
Keep reading [ https://substack.com/redirect/a0416ab5-4a83-4ab9-8c93-896de8a91dd8?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ]
NOTEBOOK
Substackers featured in this edition
Art & Photography: Erika Lee Sears , Brandon Tobiassen , Ella Wiznia , pmamtraveller  
Video & Audio: Philippa Rice  
Writing: Dara Resnik , Dia Lupo , Sachin , Jess Nash  
Recently launched
The musician Maggie Rogers  has launched her Substack: “Part-creative outlet, part-self indulgence, part-community connective tissue: this site is a place for me to document, hypothesize, criticize, vent, narrate, and wring out the excess thought-liquid swooshing around my own personal existential aquarium.”
Sonia Sodha , the former chief leader writer and columnist at The Observer, has joined Substack, where the center-left journalist will “ask the difficult questions the left too often avoids, from a nuanced and researched vantage.”
The team behind Steven Spielberg’s upcoming film Disclosure Day  has launched a Substack, sharing the trailer and promising insider access to the movie.
Universal Music has launched a Substack dedicated to celebrating Amy Winehouse ’s music, “where we revisit the stories behind the songs, the collaborators who helped bring them to life and the cultural legacy that still ripples through a new generation of artists.”
Inspired by the writers and creators featured in the Weekender? Starting your own Substack is just a few clicks away:
The Weekender is a weekly roundup of writing, ideas, art, audio, and video from the world of Substack. Posts are recommended by staff and readers, and curated and edited by Alex Posey out of Substack’s headquarters in San Francisco.
Got a Substack post to recommend? Tell us about it in the comments.

0.625em 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 1.16em;font-size: 1.375em;"><strong>That’s all, folks</strong></h3><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">As the film industry gussies up for its biggest night of the year, Dara Resnik reflects on the changing industry and what happens when Hollywood, the industry, becomes estranged from Hollywood, the place.</p><div class="captioned-image-container-static" style="font-size: 16px;line-height: 26px;margin: 32px auto;"><figure style="width: 100%;margin: 0 auto;"><table class="image-wrapper" underline;">Say Goodbye to Hollywood</a></strong></h3><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;"><span>—</span><span class="mention-pnpTE1 mentionStatic-liMklk" data-component-name="MentionStatic" style="white-space: nowrap;cursor: pointer;color: #FF7731;border-radius: 4px;transition: color .25scubic-bezier(.19,1,.22,1),background-color #ff7731;text-decoration: none;">Dara Resnik</a></span><span> in </span><span class="mention-pnpTE1 mentionStatic-liMklk" data-component-name="MentionStatic" style="white-space: nowrap;cursor: pointer;color: #FF7731;border-radius: 4px;transition: color .25scubic-bezier(.19,1,.22,1),background-color .25scubic-bezier(.19,1,.22,1),box-shadow .25scubic-bezier(.19,1,.22,1),border href="https://open.substack.com/pub/dararesnik?utm_source=mentions" style="-webkit-text-decoration: none;color: #ff7731;text-decoration: none;">Dara’s Substack</a></span><span> </span></p><blockquote style="border-left: 4px solid #FF7731;margin: 20px 0;padding: 0;"><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);margin-left: 20px;line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">After a certain number of cocktails (I’ll let you guess the over/under), several of my friends have recently turned to me at separate dinners, and sighed, exasperated: “It’s over, isn’t it?” They mean Hollywood. And by Hollywood, they mean the glamorous place and the world-famous business that grew in her womb. The bad news is that the answer is yes. The good news is that the answer is also no.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);margin-left: 20px;line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">Before the pandemic, I advised film and television writers—students and professionals—from all over the world to move to Los Angeles. That’s where the money was. That’s where the majority of the exciting jobs were. Even growing up in New York City, the Center of Planet Earth (yes, I said it), it was clear to me that if I wanted to write, produce, and/or direct tentpole films or network television, I had to move to LA. Let’s be clear: I really didn’t want to. I had a rent-controlled apartment on 86th and Broadway. I was writing for magazines. I was living a lovely version of an Upper West Side life I coveted as a kid, but I knew if I was gonna Make It, I needed to be in Hollywood.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);margin-left: 20px;line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">And I was right. Every wave of young people that moved here in a given year was essentially a college graduating “class.” The people who moved here in ’99, or ’02, or ’06 and on and on, all knew each other in some way. You’d be drinking beer in some stranger’s S’lake backyard one night and realize their roommate was the guy who had the UTA desk next to your best friend, and now he’s an agent, or the young CE you were meeting with was the assistant you used to talk to on the phone all the time when you were a PA. We rose the ranks together, because while jobs out here have always been coveted, there were just enough of them, and we brought each other up alongside us. Vacating a desk in Disney development for a promotion? You knew that girl who’d just left Barry Josephson’s company (uh-huh) would be excited to take it. Need to quit that agency mailroom gig? No problem, the network of “classmates” you arrived with had your back and there was always, within months, a gig around the corner. And while the cost of living was high, it wasn’t nearly as high as it was in the City That Never Sleeps.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);margin-left: 20px;line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">On the crew side, if you wanted to learn camera, you could start as a 2nd AC, and climb the ladder the same way. And if you got “stuck” on a rung, either as a mid-level executive, or in a crew position that wasn’t the above-the-line gig you dreamed of, you could still make a living, buy a house, get healthcare for your family, send kids to college on the salary. And the perks were enormous. Sure, maybe you’d be making the same amount of money as a middle school teacher, but you got to be on a studio lot, attend premieres, get paid for being creative in some way. You grew in an ecosystem that was creating one of America’s biggest, most lucrative exports: film and television.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);margin-left: 20px;line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;"><span>I wish I could explain to my most recent graduate students how thrilling it used to be on a studio lot. How alive it was with movement and sound and creativity. From 2001-2008, I mostly worked at Warner Bros. (first as an intern for Mimi Leder, then for </span><em>Studio 60 on the Sunset Strip</em><span>, then </span><em>Pushing Daisies</em><span>). Lunch was a trip. At one commissary table, a legendary TV director, at another, a background extra for </span><em>ER</em><span> on a break, eating Poquito Mas with a fake nail sticking out of his chest. Around the corner, wardrobe for </span><em>The West Wing</em><span> rolling by, around the next, two janitors gossiping in the Stars Hollow gazebo. Everyone was part of something bigger than themselves. While yes, the assholes still thrived because welcome to humanity, the feeling of being part of a creative community was pervasive. You couldn’t help but look wide-eyed at your colleagues sometimes and go, “Guys, we’re really DOING IT!”</span></p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);margin-left: 20px;line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">Sadly, anyone paying attention is finally admitting that has changed. Production has fled. There are fewer buyers, fewer development jobs. The studio lots are devoid of life. I recently had an in-person meeting at the Universal Studios lot, which is huge, and when I inevitably got lost, as I always have, I looked around to ask someone, ANYONE for directions, and I think I literally heard crickets. I was alone. It was eerie. Of course, there are a few productions here and there. But it’s not like it was. And it never will be again. Things change.</p></blockquote><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;"><a 0.625em 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 1.16em;font-size: 1.375em;"><strong>“They were almost home”</strong></h3><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: underline;">To be beautiful, to be memorable</a></strong></h3><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;"><span>—</span><span class="mention-pnpTE1 mentionStatic-liMklk" data-component-name="MentionStatic" style="white-space: nowrap;cursor: pointer;color: #FF7731;border-radius: 4px;transition: color .25scubic-bezier(.19,1,.22,1),background-color -.125em;padding: 0 .125em .0625em;text-decoration: none;"><a href="https://open.substack.com/users/10821767-dia-lupo?utm_source=mentions" style="-webkit-text-decoration: none;color: #ff7731;text-decoration: none;">Dia Lupo</a></span><span> in </span><a href="https://substack.com/redirect/4130e91d-e0e5-415b-be8f-6f3094ee2a79?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg" rel="" style="color: #ff7731;text-decoration: none;">Broke But Moisturized</a></p><blockquote style="border-left: 4px solid #FF7731;margin: 20px 0;padding: 0;"><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);margin-left: 20px;line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;"><em>“Women want to be loved like roses. They spend hours perfecting their eyebrows and toes and inventing irresistible curls that fall by accident down the back of their necks from otherwise austere hair-dos. They want their lover to remember the way they held a glass. They want to haunt.”</em></p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);margin-left: 20px;line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;"><em>―Eve Babitz, Slow Days, Fast Company</em></p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);margin-left: 20px;line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">Before they were called “Town Hall” meetings, they were called “All Hands.” At some point HR made us change the name because “not everyone has hands.” Believe all 60 of us had both hands but anyway, the point was to get the whole marketing team in a room once a quarter to exchange personality test results and pretend to know what EBITDA means, pretend we control such profitability metrics through Pinterest ads.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);margin-left: 20px;line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">In advance of each Town Hall, leadership would pick a few people for these “get to know the team” panels. The questions were personal, and yet everyone’s answers always circled back to work. The host would ask, “Who’s on your Spotify Wrapped this year?” and some middle manager would gush, “Like alllll classical because it helps me focus!” SHUT THE FUCK UP. JUST SAY TAYLOR SWIFT AND WALK THOSE DUSTY ROTHYS BACK TO YOUR CUBICLE.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);margin-left: 20px;line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">I remember the last Town Hall I attended before changing teams. We broke for lunch which was, invariably, a taco bar for gringos on SSRIs. Then it was panel time.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);margin-left: 20px;line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">Donna Livingston perched upon a stool in her signature glam. Her long, shiny red hair in loose curls; Disney princess eyes with 100 coats of mascara; berry lip. She embodied the bygone elegance of mid-tier city office life, the kind of woman who called a shirt a “blouse” and pants “slacks.” That day she wore black slacks and a deep-pink-hued blouse. I thought to myself, Donna loves a jewel tone. Gotta respect a woman who knows what looks good on her. I’d only ever seen Donna in sapphire and emerald and this shade of pink; it was the color of love refracting off the edges of a ruby.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);margin-left: 20px;line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">Donna worked remotely from DC. We were all used to admiring her through a screen, her dedication to Getting Ready every day to work from home. Always warm, always smiling. To see her up there on the panel reminded me that those intangibles like “grace” and “poise” can’t be fully apprehended via Microsoft Teams. Real-life Donna was just so… lovely. That’s the word. Lovely.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);margin-left: 20px;line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">At one point, the panelists were asked about their dream jobs. Everyone got fidgety because what on earth would you do if not write website copy for B2B telecom?</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);margin-left: 20px;line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">They passed Donna the mic and she turned the color of her shirt.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);margin-left: 20px;line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">“I would be a figure skater,“ she confessed, her voice shaky and girlish. “My girls skate and it’s the biggest part of our lives.”</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);margin-left: 20px;line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">Everybody loved Donna, and that was the first time most of us got a glimpse into her personal life. I must have been in a million meetings with her and she never once mentioned this thing that colored a sterile conference room with charm and wonder and truth. And then it clicked for me: that Donna’s glam might be an extension of her passion for skating. It can be a lovely thing, to look the part; it crystallizes your image in the hearts and minds of everyone who meets you.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);margin-left: 20px;line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">On January 29, 2025, American Airlines Flight 5342 collided with a US Army helicopter over the Potomac River in Washington D.C. It was a small flight, 64 people between the crew and passengers, 28 of them returning from the U.S. Figure Skating Championships training camp in Wichita, Kansas. Donna Livingston died alongside her husband Peter and their daughters, Olympic hopefuls Everly, 14, and Alydia, 11, who were known online as The Ice Skating Sisters.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);margin-left: 20px;line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">I was working from home when my friend called and broke the news. “The whole office is a mess. Everyone’s crying and leaving for the day,” she said. Meanwhile, our friend Sarah had just taken a job under Donna. Sarah was in California for a conference where Donna was to meet her when she returned from Wichita. She was texting with Donna just before she boarded Flight 5342, wished her safe travels and everything.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);margin-left: 20px;line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;"><span>What are the odds of knowing someone who dies in a 64-person plane crash? I was too catatonic for statistics but just responsive and incredulous enough to watch the video footage over and over and over. The helicopter looks like it’s being pulled directly into the plane by some invisible force, something bigger and more sinister than human error. The aircrafts collide into a magnificent ball of fire suspended in the night sky. I just kept thinking, </span><em>they were almost home.</em></p></blockquote><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;"><a href="https://substack.com/redirect/ead74483-1a7e-4db5-ab9c-b861ff7b73f7?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg" rel="" style="color: #ff7731;text-decoration: none;">Keep href="https://open.substack.com/pub/technically?utm_source=mentions" style="-webkit-text-decoration: none;color: #ff7731;text-decoration: none;">Technically</a></span><span> </span></p><blockquote style="border-left: 4px solid #FF7731;margin: 20px 0;padding: 0;"><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);margin-left: 20px;line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">Whenever a new technology arrives, the impulse is to treat it as something that has never existed before. A clean break from everything that came prior. I catch myself doing this with vibe coding constantly, and I see it everywhere around me. But the most useful lens for understanding a new phenomenon is almost never the phenomenon itself. You want something adjacent, close enough to share structural similarities but removed enough to see clearly. It’s on the lookout for something like this that I started reading more about the Maker Movement of ~2005-2015.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);margin-left: 20px;line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;"><span>The Maker Movement was the spiritual predecessor to vibe coding. The parallels are hard to miss. Vibe coding has </span><em>slop</em><span>. The Maker Movement had </span><em>crapjects</em><span>, a term the community coined for 3D-printed objects that served no purpose beyond proving you could extrude plastic into a shape. The Claude Code of that era was a $200 printer from Monoprice and a breadboard.</span></p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);margin-left: 20px;line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">The scene around making produced what were probably the first internet-native network intellectuals. Chris Anderson (who wrote the widely-read piece about the long tail) left his editor-in-chief role at Wired to start a robotics company called 3D Robotics. Cory Doctorow wrote Makers, a sci-fi novel based around characters who are hacking hardware and business models to survive in a world where everything is falling apart. These were people who gained influence by participating visibly in a making culture and writing about what it meant.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);margin-left: 20px;line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">A lot of the intellectual energy of the AI era orbits around AGI: when it arrives, what it’ll do to jobs, whether it will be aligned. The Maker Movement had its own gravitational center, and it was the idea that making physical things with your hands could produce an internal transformation. You would become more creative, more entrepreneurial, more self-reliant. The object you made mattered less than what the act of making did to you.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);margin-left: 20px;line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">In 2018, the media scholar Fred Turner published a paper that put this ideology under a microscope. His argument was that the Maker Movement had reinvented the theology of the Western Frontier for the digital age.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);margin-left: 20px;line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">The specifics of seventeenth-century Puritanism are obviously gone. Nobody at a Maker Faire was talking about predestination. But Turner traced the literary forms and the millenarian structure—the belief that a great transformation is coming, and that individual discipline will determine who makes it through. In the Maker narrative, the American landscape is economically barren. Jobs have disappeared. Institutions have failed you. And in this wilderness, the lone individual searches inside themselves for signs of the entrepreneurial spirit, the creative spark, evidence that they are among the elect who will build their way to salvation.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);margin-left: 20px;line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">Turner’s observation extends well beyond 3D printers. You can trace this same pattern through almost every hobbyist technology scene of the past fifty years. Homebrew computer clubs in the 1970s. Punk zines in the 1980s. The early web in the 1990s. Each one developed a community of practice—what Brian Eno would call a “scenius”—where people played with tools that the mainstream considered toys. Each one generated its own salvation narrative: master this tool, transform yourself, become the kind of person who builds the future.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);margin-left: 20px;line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">And each one operated with a useful kind of slack. The tools were unproductive on purpose. Nobody expected your Arduino project to ship to customers. Nobody expected your homebrew computer to compete with IBM. The whole point was that you had permission to fuck around, and the finding-out happened gradually, through play, over years. This is where the old Silicon Valley adage comes from: “What smart people do on the weekends, everyone else will do during the week in ten years.”</p></blockquote><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;"><a href="https://substack.com/redirect/ca0d7431-8b53-46ca-ac77-706a0321ba8f?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg" rel="" </span></p><blockquote style="border-left: 4px solid #FF7731;margin: 20px 0;padding: 0;"><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);margin-left: 20px;line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">The 19th-century Japanese master artist Kawanabe Kyōsai is well known for imaginative and satirical visions carried out in energetic, free brushwork. Between political caricatures, animals, ghosts, and acrobats, his scenes favour movement and lightness. It’s no surprise that he often painted birds.</p><div class="captioned-image-container-static" style="margin-left: 20px;font-size: 16px;line-height: 26px;margin: 32px auto;"><figure style="width: 100%;margin: 0 auto;"><table class="image-wrapper" width="100%" border="0" cellspacing="0" cellpadding="0" data-component-name="Image2ToDOMStatic" style="mso-padding-alt: 1em 0 1.6em;"><tbody><tr><td style="text-align: center;"></td><td class="content" align="left" width="643" style="text-align: center;"><a class="image-link" target="_blank" href="https://substack.com/redirect/f78b8751-d55b-40cd-a508-a7ad753bf56a?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg" rel="" style="position: relative;flex-direction: column;align-items: center;padding: 0;width: auto;height: auto;border: none;text-decoration: none;display: block;margin: 0;"><img class="wide-image" data-attrs="{"src":"https://substack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com/public/images/b7f1ffcf-caab-452a-9c75-cf7c18eda23e_2048x1275.png","srcNoWatermark":null,"fullscreen":null,"imageSize":null,"height":906,"width":1456,"resizeWidth":643,"bytes":null,"alt":null,"title":null,"type":null,"href":null,"belowTheFold":true,"topImage":false,"internalRedirect":null,"isProcessing":false,"align":null,"offset":false}" alt="" width="550" height="342.239010989011" src="https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!VDdw!,w_1100,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fb7f1ffcf-caab-452a-9c75-cf7c18eda23e_2048x1275.png" style="border: none !important;vertical-align: middle;display: block;-ms-interpolation-mode: bicubic;height: auto;margin-bottom: 0;width: auto !important;max-width: 100% !important;margin: 0 auto;"></a></td><td style="text-align: center;"></td></tr></tbody></table></figure></div><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);margin-left: 20px;line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">What better subjects to express liberation and motion? Kyōsai’s soaring eagles, ravens, and songbirds share their substance with moving, changing water and buoyant leaves. Their bodies are as free and flexible as the wind. Air passes through their feathers.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);margin-left: 20px;line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">Furthest from this lightness among birds is the ostrich—three hundred pounds and fully grounded. Though they share a class, an ostrich would never fit into a Kyōsai picture like a raven or an eagle does.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);margin-left: 20px;line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">This is a thick and gawky animal, strutting on heavy legs, with feathers like hair draping thickly over the bulky body and spraying out at the tail. Up the curve of the neck, there are long black eyelashes, big black eyes, and big black nostrils set into a squat beak. Besides the beak and feathers, not much about the ostrich really looks birdlike at all. They have wings, of course, but these are instrumental in running, not flying.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);margin-left: 20px;line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">“In some points it resembles a bird, in others a quadruped,” Aristotle wrote of the ostrich in the 4th-century B.C.E. biological study Parts of Animals. He could make no clear sense of what he observed: feathers and hair, two bird legs and a quadruped’s size, wings and no flight. In his taxonomy of animals, the ostrich was ultimately neither avian nor beast. It was classifiable only as “intermediate”. Indeed, many languages reflect a hybrid view of the ostrich: the Greek term strouthokámilos, the Persian shotormorq, the Turkish devekuşu, and the Chinese tuóniǎo each describe a “camel-bird.”</p><div auto;"></a></td><td style="text-align: center;"></td></tr></tbody></table></figure></div><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);margin-left: 20px;line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">Though post-Aristotle Jewish and Islamic scholars viewed the ostrich unambiguously as a bird, it was clearly not like other birds. It ate strange things; it built unusual nests; most pressingly, it didn’t fly. In Job 39:13-17, God speaks: “The wings of the ostrich wave proudly / But are her wings and pinions like the kindly stork’s?” The stork has wings that, as biology professor Joel Duff reads, “are beautiful and enable flight.” Not the ostrich.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);margin-left: 20px;line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">We on foot have always coveted the wide space of the sky, and the natural ease with which birds navigate it. A bird seems to be a light and free spirit, unconfined by its nature. The caging of a bird is a serious cruelty, and doves soar at weddings. A winged human is nothing less than an angel.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);margin-left: 20px;line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;"><em>(Burst the wild storm? above it thou ascended’st, / And rested on the sky, thy slave that cradled thee) / Now a blue point, far, far in heaven floating</em></p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);margin-left: 20px;line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;"><em>—Walt Whitman (To the Man-of-War-Bird)</em></p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);margin-left: 20px;line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">“The human fascination with flight seems to be associated with our desire for the pure transcendence of art,” writes poet Rosemarie Corlett. Leonardo da Vinci, who worked obsessively at building a flying machine, loved birds; he used to buy them at the market so that he could set them free. Wilbur Wright, of the brothers who finally saw the dream of aviation through, wrote:</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);margin-left: 20px;line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;"><em>The desire to fly is an idea handed down to us by our ancestors who, in their grueling travels across trackless lands in prehistoric times, looked enviously on the birds soaring freely through space, at full speed, above all obstacles, on the infinite highway of the air.</em></p></blockquote><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;"><a Give Your Team the Insights Without the Legwork

We heard you. You love MIT SMR research, but getting those ideas in front of your team or leadership in a way they’d actually engage with was a challenge. So we built something to close that gap: MIT SMR Action Packs.

Each Action Pack pairs a full MIT SMR article with a ready-to-present deck of key insights and takeaways. Here are five you can share with your team and start applying the insights today.

⬇️

message.subject: The Bailout that isn’t a bailout
message.snippet: A Play in three acts. On the Anthropic-Pentagon Spectacle and what it is costing The Human ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏
message.plain_text_body:
View this post on the web at https://msukhareva.substack.com/p/the-bailout-that-isnt-a-bailout

This article is a collaboration with Tony Siewert [ https://substack.com/redirect/63b0539a-7766-4fb3-9c59-36324d80056a?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ], a tech professional who dissects the cultural and philosophical impacts of modern AI. Tony champions the messy, vital human element in the age of automation and explores why, in today's tech landscape, he takes pride in being branded as 'The Antiquated One [ https://substack.com/@tonysiewert1 ]’.
Act I. The Three Actors
In the beginning of March 2026, one and a half million people switched AI providers out of moral outrage. They cancelled their subscriptions to OpenAI and signed up with Anthropic. By every available metric, it was an act of resistance. By every structural measure, it was the exact opposite.  
The stage was being built weeks and months earlier. The cast included Anthropic, OpenAI, and the Pentagon itself. On the surface, the play is about the question of Ethics: can a private company defend its safety protocols against the will of the state? The real drama, however, played out between the lines.
The first company to deploy its models [ https://substack.com/redirect/bee85137-1969-4354-ba4b-d0c298f0e371?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ] on the classified networks of the US government was Anthropic, our actor number one. Their Claude Models were already running inside mission-critical intelligence operations [ https://substack.com/redirect/e97655f3-6f11-484a-9f4e-b6831cce8681?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ]. They even explicitly opened its models for missile defense and combat logistics. 
Anthropic always does everything “correctly” — almost suspiciously so.
Sounds great? We aren’t done yet. Anthropic had a product that built a genuine community. Claude Code became the primary driver [ https://substack.com/redirect/52e47849-a36c-474e-9de2-d8b690daac6e?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ] of growth velocity and valuation at Anthropic. The latest models — Opus 4.6 and Sonnet 4.6 — were leading the major benchmarks in AI, LMSYS Arena [ https://substack.com/redirect/e010ec3d-71f3-4ff5-92d0-495faafa9f5a?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ] among them.
That is the definition of Aura Farming, but even that wasn’t enough. When literally dealing with the Ministry of War, Anthropic managed to maintain two red lines: 1) Claude would not be used for mass surveillance of American citizens [ https://substack.com/redirect/4980675b-526c-40e3-8945-5c2cf9725236?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ]. 2) And it would not control fully autonomous lethal weapon systems. The first line was unambiguously ethical. The second existed for a slightly simpler reason: Dario Amodei genuinely did not believe current AI models were ready for that use case [ https://substack.com/redirect/88286781-af49-471f-b279-b9e2f408b5f3?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ]. 
One might expect that doing everything “correctly” earns a reward. But Actor number 2 has a mind of his own: Enter the Pentagon. Following the general ultra-conservative rollback, the Pentagon was already unhappy about “woke” AI [ https://substack.com/redirect/f2078937-b21f-4c19-ac39-81206eb968f8?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ] in January 2026. Weeks of tense negotiations over a $200 million contract followed. On February 24th, Defense Secretary Peter Hegseth delivered an ultimatum to Amodei in person: drop all AI safety rules by Friday, February 27 — or be designated a “supply-chain risk to national security”.
No American company had ever been threatened with such treatment — it is normally reserved for cases like Huawei, a subsidiary of a geopolitical rival. The threat works not through legal precision but through the ambiguity it creates. Though the designation formally applied only to direct military contracts, defense contractors and suppliers are heavily incentivized to drop Anthropic’s products preemptively, if only to avoid the associated stigma. For a company whose models are already deeply embedded in these infrastructures, the designation alone can be existential.
Anthropic — the company that does everything “correctly” — refused to comply and challenged the designation in court. Trump himself weighed in with a post branding Anthropic a “leftwing nutjob” and worse. Anthropic stood alone.
The post [ https://substack.com/redirect/38531cc7-6420-42c0-97c0-bcb3a5e2ba86?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ] of Donald Trump on Anthropic
Act II. The Bailout That Isn’t a Bailout
Anyone who thinks Anthropic brought this upon itself has forgotten the third player: OpenAI with its CEO Sam Altman.
Unlike Anthropic, OpenAI is in serious trouble. The company that launched the LLM hype with the massive success of ChatGPT has become the central node in a web of circular financing deals that artificially prop up large parts [ https://substack.com/redirect/d5ca7987-62ca-47e7-92cd-44d501506b18?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ] of the US tech economy. When Microsoft “invests” in OpenAI only for that money to flow directly back as payment for Azure services, what emerges can hardly be described as value chain as it mimicks a carousel. That such constructions blur the line between creative accounting and antitrust evasion is no secret [ https://substack.com/redirect/06c1f260-657f-49de-aebb-5152f1390a1f?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ].
And as tech critic Ed Zitron recently revealed through leaked financial documents, the subsequent revenue numbers that OpenAI has shown to the world are failing to convince anyone that the ‘math is mathing [ https://substack.com/redirect/28a86548-9cda-463c-9f81-53c7c73c3bac?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ]’.
The deals have grown so vast that OpenAI is already being labeled [ https://substack.com/redirect/22817f9c-44d6-4813-bdd6-de2977b92039?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ] “too big to fail” — and the question of a state-guaranteed bailout hangs openly in the air. Set against Anthropic’s success — better products, no absurd debt mountains, everything done “correctly” — OpenAI’s position turns precarious.
When customers began defecting en masse to Google and flagship model underperformed, the company declared “Code Red” in December 2025 — halting most side projects overnight in a desperate pivot to salvage its core technology.
No AI drama of this era unfolds without OpenAI — this one least of all. First, Sam Altman publicly backed Amodei’s principled stance — despite having refused to shake his hand at the AI summit in New Delhi just weeks earlier.
Sam Altman refuses to take the hand with Dario Amodei (third and second from the right, respectrively)
Then, immediately after the Pentagon branded Anthropic a supply-chain risk, Altman announced two things. OpenAI would step into the Pentagon contract. And it had secured a historic $110 billion funding round. Both on the same day. The entire Anthropic-Pentagon narrative was instantly subsumed under a single name: Sam Altman.
OpenAI’s sudden alignment with the military becomes legible against this backdrop. In late 2025, the administration had explicitly refused a direct bailout — as there were enough frontier companies, and they were allowed to fail. OpenAI was out of options.
By taking the Pentagon contract OpenAI established a new state-secured monopoly. And that is, essentially, a bailout that isn’t a bailout.
Yet Altman wouldn’t be Altman if Anthropic were simply thrown under the bus. In the end, Anthropic may have been the biggest winner of the entire spectacle. Anthropic’s refusal is being celebrated as a second coming of Apple’s [ https://substack.com/redirect/dc66a589-211c-4758-b6fc-567c1542654f?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ] famous decision not to install backdoors on its devices. Downloads surged across [ https://substack.com/redirect/7b2c0410-2dbd-450e-9ef6-143710d992c0?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ] every app store. Millions switched from ChatGPT to Claude.
What began as a meme quickly became a measurable exodus. Under the banner of #QuitGPT [ https://substack.com/redirect/eb0b3741-a64d-4da9-bf64-a9d35678b2f3?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ], over 1.5 million users pledged to boycott OpenAI, driving a 295% spike in ChatGPT uninstalls and propelling Claude to number one on the App Store.
Amodei was declared a hero. The Atlantic even published an article [ https://substack.com/redirect/8d1b1374-f4b0-4494-97a4-9a77c51e15c1?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ], directly comparing Dario Amodei with Oppenheimer. 
Act III. The Absence of the Human
And yet, on this whole stage, there is but one reality: none of it truly matters.
OpenAI may well be glad to shed its freeloaders — the free user base was nearly impossible to monetize. But even beyond such speculation: most consumer AI subscriptions are massively subsidized — by venture capital, by Big Tech, by one of the largest corporate subsidization campaigns in the history of technology. Even the most expensive Claude Max Subscription may consume [ https://substack.com/redirect/b11e6f92-cc26-4b3a-8ec5-5f8bcce0f1c6?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ] up to $5.000 worth of compute.   
It has never been about short-term profit, as none of these companies can cover their astronomical computing costs even remotely. The true goal is infrastructure capture. They want to be the default platform—meaning their models become the foundational layer that every other company, developer, and government agency is forced to build upon. They are giving away billions in subsidized compute today to ensure they own the monopolies of tomorrow. This is the field on which the entire theatre takes place.
In this play, there can only be one winner: technology itself. And the principal loser is conspicuous by absence: The Human. In no act of this spectacle did The Human ever appear as subject. The Pentagon was only about securing its perceived dominance. OpenAI fought merely to survive its debt web.
And Anthropic? The ethical AI company — which assembled its training data from the intellectual property of the world [ https://substack.com/redirect/d36db786-11a0-4f86-ba24-68ac990053b3?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ], while only a privileged few creators can afford the legal battle against such a giant. Before the Pentagon drama, Anthropic had already quietly lowered the safety requirements [ https://substack.com/redirect/14248f4a-561f-40ac-b9a5-eeddc3d55cfb?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ] for its next generation of models.
And the red lines? The recent mass killing of at least 160 schoolgirls during the strike on Iran was probably caused by a faulty target identification [ https://substack.com/redirect/53986821-3378-4bb1-bab0-e5ea1a14e33a?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ] by Claude’s models. This happened one day after Amodei had been celebrated as the ethical conscience of the AI industry.
Anthropic is far closer to OpenAI in spirit than either would ever admit. They need each other. One is the villain whose presence lets the other’s virtue shine. “Thank God there’s only one Sam Altman!” critics may say — while both companies jointly industrialize what Günther Anders once diagnosed as the ground condition of our epoch: the obsolescence of the human in the face of its own creations.
If all of this is theatre, if the bailout is no bailout, then the play functions not as an attack on the human, which would presuppose intent, but as something staged past the human entirely, in complete structural indifference. 
The consumer’s decision to switch to Anthropic changes nothing about the underlying dynamic, even though it feels like resistance. The hand still reaches. What has been replaced is everything behind it.
The true achievement of this spectacle: it takes the genuine moral outrage of its users, their still-functioning ethical intuition, and converts it into a new subscription. The Industrialization of Hype. In this apparatus, Anthropic can never be seen as the antidote. It is merely other side of the same mechanism. What it produces is the ultimate obsolescence: our deepest moral convictions, reduced to raw material for the expansion of the very system they were meant to oppose.
In the end, the entire spectacle of AI ethics revealed itself as what it was from the start: a mechanism for securing backdoor bailouts — and for accelerating the very machinery that no longer requires the human it was supposedly about.

Unsubscribe https://substack..tfkPcA37S8gfUWXa5n7jl82sYHjrhwPENsQ_Jk9kVSo?

20px;font-weight: 400;letter-spacing: -.15px;margin-top: 8px;width: 70%;padding-left: 15%;padding-right: 15%;text-align: center;">Anthropic is making Human Obsolescence Great again. Painting by Zdzislaw Beksinski.</figcaption></figure></div><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;"><span>This article is a collaboration with </span><a href="https://substack.com/redirect/63b0539a-7766-4fb3-9c59-36324d80056a?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg" rel="" style="color: rgb(54,55,55);text-decoration: underline;">Tony Siewert</a><span>, a tech professional who dissects the cultural and philosophical impacts of modern AI. Tony champions the messy, vital human element in the age of automation and explores why, in today's tech landscape, he takes pride in being branded as '</span><a href="https://substack.com/@tonysiewert1" rel="" style="color: rgb(54,55,55);text-decoration: underline;">The Antiquated One</a><span>’.</span></p><h1 class="header-anchor-post" style="position: relative;font-family: 'SF Pro Display',-apple-system-headline,system-ui,-apple-system,BlinkMacSystemFont,'Segoe UI',Roboto,Helvetica,Arial,sans-serif,'Apple Color Emoji','Segoe UI Emoji','Segoe UI Symbol';font-weight: bold;-webkit-font-smoothing: antialiased;-moz-osx-font-smoothing: antialiased;-webkit-appearance: optimizelegibility;-moz-appearance: optimizelegibility;appearance: optimizelegibility;margin: 1em 0 0.625em 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 1.16em;font-size: 2em;">Act I. The Three Actors</h1><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;"><span>In the beginning of March 2026, one and a half million people switched AI providers out of moral outrage. They cancelled their subscriptions to OpenAI and signed up with Anthropic. By every available metric, it was an act of resistance. By every structural measure, it was the exact opposite. </span><strong> </strong></p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;"><span>The stage was being built weeks and months earlier. The cast included </span><strong>Anthropic</strong><span>, </span><strong>OpenAI</strong><span>, and the </span><strong>Pentagon</strong><span> itself. On the surface, the play is about the question of Ethics: can a private company defend its safety protocols against the will of the state? The real drama, however, played out between the lines.</span></p><div class="subscribe-widget is-signed-up" data-component-name="SubscribeWidget" style="margin: 0 0 1em;direction: ltr;font-size: 16px;line-height: 26px;"><div class="pencraft pc-reset button-wrapper" style="text-decoration: unset;list-style: none;font-size: 16px;line-height: 26px;text-align: center;cursor: pointer;border-radius: 4px;"><a class="button subscribe-btn primary" href="https://substack.com/redirect/2/eyJlIjoiaHR0cHM6Ly9tc3VraGFyZXZhLnN1YnN0YWNrLmNvbS9zdWJzY3JpYmU_.dHmAc8vJZum8gCh8d-ZvhXjnfHgLXNEZGaCXIwPLGuQ?&utm_medium=email&utm_source=subscribe-widget&utm_content=190762452" style="font-family: system-ui,-apple-system,BlinkMacSystemFont,'Segoe UI',Roboto,Helvetica,Arial,sans-serif,'Apple Color Emoji','Segoe UI Emoji','Segoe UI Symbol';display: inline-block;box-sizing: border-box;cursor: pointer;border: none;border-radius: 8px;font-size: 14px;line-height: 20px;font-weight: 600;text-align: center;opacity: 1;outline: none;white-space: nowrap;text-decoration: none !important;margin: 0 auto;background-color: #f87853;color: #ffffff !important;padding: 12px 20px;height: auto;"><span style="color: #ffffff;text-decoration: none;">Upgrade to paid</span></a></div></div><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;"><span>The first company to</span><a href="https://substack.com/redirect/bee85137-1969-4354-ba4b-d0c298f0e371?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg" rel="" style="color: rgb(54,55,55);text-decoration: underline;"> deploy its models</a><span> on the classified networks of the US government was Anthropic, our actor number one. Their Claude Models were already running inside </span><a href="https://substack.com/redirect/e97655f3-6f11-484a-9f4e-b6831cce8681?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg" rel="" style="color: rgb(54,55,55);text-decoration: underline;">mission-critical intelligence operations</a><span>. They even explicitly opened its models for missile defense and combat logistics. </span></p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;"><em>Anthropic always does everything “correctly” — almost suspiciously so.</em></p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;"><span>Sounds great? We aren’t done yet. Anthropic had a product that built a genuine community. Claude Code became the </span><a href="https://substack.com/redirect/52e47849-a36c-474e-9de2-d8b690daac6e?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg" rel="" style="color: rgb(54,55,55);text-decoration: underline;">primary driver</a><span> of growth velocity and valuation at Anthropic. The latest models — Opus 4.6 and Sonnet 4.6 — were leading the major benchmarks in AI, </span><a href="https://substack.com/redirect/e010ec3d-71f3-4ff5-92d0-495faafa9f5a?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg" rel="" style="color: rgb(54,55,55);text-decoration: underline;">LMSYS Arena</a><span> among them.</span></p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;"><span>That is the definition of </span><em>Aura Farming, </em><span>but even that wasn’t enough. When literally dealing with the Ministry of War, Anthropic managed to maintain two red lines: 1) Claude would not be used for mass</span><a href="https://substack.com/redirect/4980675b-526c-40e3-8945-5c2cf9725236?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg" rel="" style="color: rgb(54,55,55);text-decoration: underline;"> surveillance of American citizens</a><span>. 2) And it would not control fully autonomous lethal weapon systems. The first line was unambiguously ethical. The second existed for a slightly simpler reason: Dario Amodei genuinely did not believe current AI models were </span><a href="https://substack.com/redirect/88286781-af49-471f-b279-b9e2f408b5f3?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg" rel="" style="color: rgb(54,55,55);text-decoration: underline;">ready for that use case</a><span>. </span></p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;"><span>One might expect that doing everything “correctly” earns a reward. But Actor number 2 has a mind of his own: Enter the Pentagon. Following the general ultra-conservative rollback, the Pentagon was already</span><a href="https://substack.com/redirect/f2078937-b21f-4c19-ac39-81206eb968f8?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg" rel="" style="color: rgb(54,55,55);text-decoration: underline;"> unhappy about “woke” AI</a><span> in January 2026. Weeks of tense negotiations over a $200 million contract followed. On February 24th, Defense Secretary Peter Hegseth delivered an ultimatum to Amodei in person: drop all AI safety rules by Friday, February 27 — or be designated a “supply-chain risk to national security”.</span></p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">No American company had ever been threatened with such treatment — it is normally reserved for cases like Huawei, a subsidiary of a geopolitical rival. The threat works not through legal precision but through the ambiguity it creates. Though the designation formally applied only to direct military contracts, defense contractors and suppliers are heavily incentivized to drop Anthropic’s products preemptively, if only to avoid the associated stigma. For a company whose models are already deeply embedded in these infrastructures, the designation alone can be existential.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">Anthropic — the company that does everything “correctly” — refused to comply and challenged the designation in court. Trump himself weighed in with a post branding Anthropic a “leftwing nutjob” and worse. Anthropic stood alone.</p><div class="captioned-image-container-static" style="font-size: 16px;line-height: 26px;margin: 32px auto;"><figure style="width: 100%;margin: 0 auto;"><table rgb(54,55,55);text-decoration: underline;">post</a><span> of Donald Trump on Anthropic</span></p><div style="font-size: 16px;line-height: 26px;"><hr style="margin: 32px 0;padding: 0;height:
